description: Real world case report from PMC (Scrubbed)
target_diagnosis:
Pulmonary embolism is not mentioned in this case.

The primary presenting diagnosis is:

Poorly differentiated serous papillary cystadenocarcinoma (umbilical mass)

However, the final conclusion after extensive investigation is:

Ovarian cancer stage 4 

(Note: The original diagnosis was based on the umbilical mass, but subsequent investigations led to the conclusion that the primary site was likely ovarian cancer)
vignette:
A 59-year-old Caucasian woman was admitted to our hospital with a progressively enlarging but painless mass in the region of the umbilicus, which developed two months after liver transplantation. Physical examination revealed a firm nodule with irregular borders, wine-red shade, and fixed to surrounding tissues. Overlying skin was not ulcerated.

Laboratory tests showed:

* Hemoglobin level: 9.6 g/dl
* Total bilirubin: 0.6 mg/dl
* Conjugated bilirubin: 0.1 mg/dl
* Alkaline Phosphates: 273 U/l
* GGT: 40 U/L
* AST: 36 U/L
* ALT: 56 U/L

Ultrasound of the abdominal cavity showed a 4 × 4 cm mass below the umbilicus with additional smaller nodules confined to the lower abdominal wall. Plain anterior/posterior chest x-ray was normal.

A CT scan and trans-vaginal ultrasonography did not show any lesion that could be considered a primary source of the metastasis to the umbilicus. Tumor marker evaluation showed:

* CEA: 0.22 ng/ml
* Alpha feto protein: 1.49 UI/ml
* CA125: 27.73 UI/ml
* CA 19.9: 8.61 UI/ml
* CA 15.3: 22.48 UI/ml

seed_nodes.claim: Progressively enlarging painless mass in the region of the umbilicus

description: Real world case report from PMC (Scrubbed)
vignette:
A 23-year-old male patient presents to the clinic with a single cutaneous abscess on the right side of his neck, which has been present for 6 months. He also reports cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy. His HIV status is negative, and initial chest X-ray (CXR) shows no abnormalities.

Physical examination reveals:

* A single cutaneous abscess on the right side of the neck
* Cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy

Initial laboratory results include:

* Normal sputum samples for acid-fast bacilli (AFB)
* No evidence of tuberculosis in three consecutive spot-early morning-spot sputum samples
* Aspirated pus from the cutaneous abscess shows plenty of M. tuberculosis on culture, confirmed by niacin and nitrate reduction tests, rapid bacteriophage assay, and RFLP analysis.

What is your next step in diagnosing this patient?

seed_nodes.claim: Single cutaneous abscess on the right side of the neck

description: Real world case report from PMC (Scrubbed)
vignette:
A 75-year-old diabetic male presents with dull upper abdominal pain of one-week duration and swelling in the left upper abdomen. He has no history of vomiting, fever, or gastrointestinal bleeding. His physical exam reveals a large mass palpable in the epigastrium and left hypochondrium.

Routine lab results are normal. An ultrasound and CT scan of the abdomen show a large heterogeneous mass extending from the tail of the pancreas to the anterior pararenal space, involving the lesser sac and gastrosplenic ligament.

What is your differential diagnosis for this patient's abdominal mass?

seed_nodes.claim: dull upper abdominal pain of one-week duration

description: Real world case report from PMC (Scrubbed)
target_diagnosis: Poorly differentiated mucinous adenocarcinoma of colon
vignette:
A 24-year-old woman presents to the surgical clinic with a 3-month history of:

* Epigastric pain
* Feculent vomiting
* Post-prandial diarrhea
* Significant weight loss (over one stone)

She is previously healthy and not taking any regular medications.

Physical exam reveals:

* No abdominal scars or tenderness

Initial lab results are:

* Hemoglobin: 9.5 g/dL
* Mean cell volume: 71.6 fl
* White blood cell count: 20.2 × 10^9/L
* Normal urea, electrolytes, and liver function tests

An ultrasound scan of the abdomen shows:

* Thickened bowel in the right upper quadrant
* Dilated duodenum

A barium meal and follow-through demonstrates:

* Mucosal abnormality on the greater curvature of the stomach
* Fistulous tract into the transverse colon (Figure)

Imaging to be considered:

description: Real world case report from PMC (Scrubbed)
vignette:
A 55-year-old woman with a history of smoking presents to the emergency department with symptoms. On physical examination, she appears to be in respiratory distress.

Initial labs:

* Complete Blood Count: WNL
* Basic Metabolic Panel: WNL
* Chest X-ray: Bilateral pulmonary nodules

CT scan of the chest demonstrates bilateral pulmonary nodules.

What is your next step?

description: Real world case report from PMC (Scrubbed)
vignette:
A 31-year-old man presents with a painless left inguinal tumor that has gradually grown over the past six months. Physical examination reveals a fist-sized subcutaneous tumor in the inguinal region, with normal-appearing skin overlying it and no inguinal lymphadenopathy.

What is your initial impression of this case?

The patient undergoes a computed tomography (CT) scan, which shows a solid tumor with heterogeneous contrast enhancement in the adipose tissue. There are no metastases to the liver or lung.

Review the CT images:

* Computed Tomography: _______________________________________________________

Which first-line investigations and imaging studies would you order next?

description: Real world case report from PMC (Scrubbed)
target_diagnosis:
Dilated aortic root
Enlarged right atrium 
Pulsing flail of the fossa ovalis membrane 

Note: The actual acute primary diagnosis might be more specific, but based on the provided text, these are the extracted conditions related to the specific episode that seemed critical for the clinicians' decision-making.
vignette:
**Diagnostic Challenge**

A 70-year-old male patient presents with paroxysmal atrial fibrillation and atrial flutter, as well as concomitant arterial hypertension. On physical exam:

* What are the key findings from the physical examination?

Initial labs and imaging:

* The preprocedural TEE revealed an aneurysm of the IAS.
* ICE performed during the electrophysiology laboratory showed:
	+ Dilated aortic root
	+ Enlarged right atrium displacing the IAS towards the LA free wall
	+ Small oval fossa with flapping motion of the fossa ovalis membrane
	+ Close proximity between the IAS and LA free wall during atrial diastole, making transseptal puncture potentially hazardous

seed_nodes.claim: paroxysmal atrial fibrillation and atrial flutter

description: Real world case report from PMC (Scrubbed)
vignette:
A 33-year-old male presents to the emergency department with a 3-month history of decreased vision in the left eye and left-sided headache. Physical exam reveals left eye blindness with primary optic atrophy and no other neurological deficits.

Initial labs: none

Imaging:

* Bony tumor in the left ethmoid sinus
* Invasion into the left orbit, compressing the left optic nerve
* Intracranial extension into the anterior cranial fossa on the left side

seed_nodes.claim: Decreased vision in the left eye

description: Real world case report from PMC (Scrubbed)
target_diagnosis:
Here is the acute, primary presenting diagnosis:

Appendicitis 

However, it's worth noting that this was initially suspected but later proved to be secondary to a more complex underlying condition.

The actual underlying and primary cause was:

Hernia (specifically, diaphragmatic hernia with intrathoracic colon displacement)
vignette:
**Diagnostic Challenge**

A 43-year-old female district nurse presents to the emergency department with:

* 6-hour history of generalized abdominal pain localizing to the right, associated with nausea and loss of appetite
* No history of recent or past trauma to the chest or abdomen
* Physical examination reveals:
	+ Tenderness in the right upper quadrant and right iliac fossa with rebound and guarding
	+ Rovsing positive sign
	+ Normal bowel sounds
* Incidental findings include:
	+ Marfanoid features (long span of upper limbs, high-arched palate, soft early diastolic murmur of aortic regurgitation)
	+ Positive family history of Marfan's syndrome

Initial laboratory and imaging results show:

* Pyrexia
* Raised white cell count
* Elevated C-reactive protein
* Abdominal X-ray: absence of gas in the right side of her bowel
* Chest X-ray: no obvious abnormality

What is your next step in this patient's management?

seed_nodes.claim: Generalized abdominal pain localizing to the right with nausea and loss of appetite

description: Real world case report from PMC (Scrubbed)
target_diagnosis:
Here is the acute, primary presenting diagnosis:

Acute cholecystitis
vignette:
A 32-year-old female is admitted with a 3-hour history of epigastric pain radiating into her back, accompanied by vomiting. She had similar episodes in the previous week. On examination, she has no jaundice or pyrexia, but does have:

* Epigastric tenderness
* Apex beat at the right 5th intercostal space, midclavicular line
* No tenderness in the right or left upper quadrants

Initial lab results show:

* Normal white cell count and amylase level
* Elevated C-reactive protein (CRP) level of 290 mg/L

An electrocardiograph reveals:

* Right axis deviation
* Right ventricular hypertrophy, consistent with dextrocardia

Imaging studies include an ultrasound scan showing the gallbladder in the left upper quadrant with stones, and the spleen in the right upper quadrant. There is no evidence of common bile duct or intrahepatic duct dilatation on the ultrasound. A chest X-Ray confirms the diagnosis of dextrocardia.
